summary: Gruber dissects Apple's claim that the iPhone 'runs OS X,' addressing two opposing misconceptions: that it runs a full desktop Mac OS X, and that the claim is marketing BS. He explains that the iPhone runs a genuine subset of OS X — with the Mach kernel, Darwin underpinnings, and Cocoa frameworks — but with a completely new UI designed for a mobile device. He draws a useful distinction between 'OS X' as the underlying technology platform and 'Mac OS X' as the specific desktop product, arguing the iPhone claim is more accurate from an engineering perspective than a marketing one. He extends this analysis to Apple TV and explores the branding implications for Windows users and the Mac brand.
key_points: The iPhone runs a genuine but heavily trimmed subset of OS X — Mach kernel, Darwin, Cocoa AppKit — not a full Mac OS X installation, which would be far too large at ~2GB vs the iPhone's ~500MB OS.

Slashdot-style skepticism that the iPhone can't be running OS X (based on Darwin licensing and ARM support arguments) is wrong because Apple owns the Darwin copyright and can do whatever it wants with its own code.
The distinction Gruber draws: 'OS X' is the technology (kernel, drivers, frameworks), while 'Mac OS X' is the product (OS X plus Mac-specific apps and UI). The iPhone runs OS X but not Mac OS X.

The iPhone UI shares nothing with the Mac UI — no menu bar, no windows, no mouse cursor — so from a lay user's perspective it neither looks nor acts like a Mac, even though engineers would recognize it as OS X.
Apple's branding strategy deliberately separates iPhone from 'Mac' to avoid scaring Windows users and to keep the iPhone floating above the Mac-vs-Windows rivalry, much like the iPod.
money_quotes: It's more accurate to call the iPhone's system software 'OS X' from an engineering perspective than from a marketing one.
Mac nerds, and Cocoa programmers in particular, would look at the iPhone's system and agree, Holy crap, that really is Mac OS X.
It's a 'full' version of OS X not because it contains everything from Mac OS X, but because it contains everything you'd actually want from Mac OS X.

There is no menu bar. There are no windows. There is no mouse cursor. These three things have been the defining fundamental elements of the Mac UI ever since the original Macintosh, and none are present in the iPhone UI.
OS X is technology; Mac OS X is a product.
You wouldn't want to take a UI optimized for 20-inch displays and squish it onto a 3.5-inch screen.

On a technical level, yes, an iPhone does appear to be much more like a Mac than an iPod. But on a marketing level, the iPhone is very much like an iPod, floating high above the fray of the 20-year-old Mac-vs.-Windows rivalry.
key_insight: The iPhone legitimately runs OS X in the computer science sense — same kernel, same frameworks — but Apple's careful distinction between 'OS X' the technology and 'Mac OS X' the product reveals a deliberate platform strategy that extends beyond the Mac.
